coredata.dc:title: Fluorimetric ratiometric tryptophan detection based on dual-ultraviolet-emission fluorine-doped carbon nanodots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Kai-Qi
Wang, Zhong-Xia
Jiang, Zhen-Tao
Li, Feng
Li, Heng-Ye
Wang, Wei
Gao, Hang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, strong electronegativity fluorine-doped carbon nanodots (FCDs) with distinctive dual-ultraviolet-emission characteristics was easily synthesized by a simple solvothermal approach using 4-fluorophenethylamine as the only precursor. Experimental results demonstrate that the FCDs exhibits dual-ultraviolet luminous peaks at 286 and 386 nm due to the participation of fluorine and nitrogen atoms, under 265 nm excitation. Next, the FCDs can be further served as an efficacious ratiometric photoluminescence (PL) probe for tryptophan (Trp) analysis based on the energy/electron transfer of the inner filter effect mechanism between FCDs and Trp. Under optimized experimental conditions, a wide linear relationship obtained between the PL386nm/PL286nm ratio of FCDs and the concentrations of Trp in the range of 0–200.0 μM, with the limit of detection of 0.31 µM (3σ/k). Moreover, the developed ratiometric probe was smoothly applied for estimating Trp in serum samples, revealing promising application opportunities in biological fluids. This work further demonstrates the importance of heteroatom species in regulating the physicochemical properties of carbon materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorine doping
Dual-ultraviolet-emission
Tryptophan
Ratiometric detection
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Investigation of the photophysical performance of the FCDs
Ratiometric detection of Trp using FCDs-based sensing platform

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21974120, 22204075), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20191479, BK20220769), the Project of State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2309), the Jiangsu Province for the College Graduate Research and Innovation Projects (KYCX23_3470), and the Funding for School-Level Research Projects of Yancheng Institute of Technology (xjr2022012).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A), FT-IR measurement (B), XPS full survey (C), C1s (D), N1s (E), O1s (F) and F1s (G) spectra of the FCDs. Inset (A): HRTEM image of the FCDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption (curve a), excitation (curve b, λex = 265 nm) and emission (curve c) spectra of the FCDs. (B) PL spectra of the FCDs at various excitations from 245 to 295 nm, set the excitation and emission slits to 5 nm respectively.

(A) PL spectra of FCDs in the existence of various concentrations of Trp (0 to 300 μM); (B) Related linearity between the signal of PL386nm/PL286nm and the Trp concentration; (C) PL spectra of FCDs in the existence of Trp or other interfering substances; (D) Selectivity of the FCDs-based sensing platform. CTrp was 200 μM and Cinterferents were all 300 μM.

(A) The excitation (curve a, λex = 265 nm) and emission (curve b) spectra of the FCDs; and the excitation (curve c, λex = 286 nm) and emission (curve d) spectra of the Trp; (B) Decay of the PL of the FCDs in the nonexistence and existence of Trp (100 µM), respectively.
The principle of the proposed Trp analysis concept.
Results of the determination of Trp in BSS (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Control the concentration of Trp in real samples within the range of 0–200 µM before analysis.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The data were obtained from three parallel samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The summarizing PL lifetimes and their parameters of the FCDs and the FCDs/Trp.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Where τ and τ0 are all the PL lifetimes of FCDs in the nonexistence and existence of Trp.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Calculate τAvg. based on the formula of τAvg. = ΣAiτi.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The PL change efficiency (CE) is obtained based on the formula of CE = 1 – τ/τ0.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The rate of electron transfer (kET) is obtained based on the formula of kET = 1/τ – 1/τ0.

head.ce:title: Fluorimetric ratiometric tryptophan detection based on dual-ultraviolet-emission fluorine-doped carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Kai-Qi Liu and Zhong-Xia Wang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, strong electronegativity fluorine-doped carbon nanodots (FCDs) with distinctive dual-ultraviolet-emission characteristics was easily synthesized by a simple solvothermal approach using 4-fluorophenethylamine as the only precursor. Experimental results demonstrate that the FCDs exhibits dual-ultraviolet luminous peaks at 286 and 386 nm due to the participation of fluorine and nitrogen atoms, under 265 nm excitation. Next, the FCDs can be further served as an efficacious ratiometric photoluminescence (PL) probe for tryptophan (Trp) analysis based on the energy/electron transfer of the inner filter effect mechanism between FCDs and Trp. Under optimized experimental conditions, a wide linear relationship obtained between the PL386nm/PL286nm ratio of FCDs and the concentrations of Trp in the range of 0–200.0 μM, with the limit of detection of 0.31 µM (3σ/k). Moreover, the developed ratiometric probe was smoothly applied for estimating Trp in serum samples, revealing promising application opportunities in biological fluids. This work further demonstrates the importance of heteroatom species in regulating the physicochemical properties of carbon materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tryptophan (Trp), as a necessary amino acid, is indeed not naturally produced by the human body and must acquire from dietary sources [1]. Its role as a precursor for essential compounds like niacin, melatonin and serotonin in the human body highlights its importance in regulating mood, sleep and mental health [2,3]. These functions are fundamental to its contribution in maintaining overall health and preventing various diseases. Therefore, it's important to maintain a balanced diet that includes Trp-rich foods such as chicken, fish, eggs, dairy products, nuts and seeds, to ensure that the body has an adequate supply of this essential Trp. However, an abnormal level of Trp can result in delusions and hallucinations, increasing the risk of conditions like neurodegenerative and schizophrenia diseases such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson's syndrome diseases [4,5]. Moreover, endogenous Trp plays also a crucial role as a diagnostic signal for hepatocellular disease [3,6]. Consequently, there is a substantial interested in the development of a straightforward, highly sensitive and specific method for Trp detection, with applications in both disease diagnosis and nutritional analysis of foods.

Up to now, various methods have been employed to measure Trp, including gas chromatography [7], colorimetric sensing [8], high-performance liquid chromatography [9] and electrochemical sensing [10–12], etc. While these methods are known for their reliability and effectiveness in various biological analyses, they do have certain drawbacks including extended detection time, the requirements for expensive equipment and complex analytical procedures. To overcome the shortcomings of the above-mentioned traditional methods, an innovative approach should be developed as a traditional alternative method. Recently, fluorometric assay has aroused increasing interest among researchers due to their high selectivity and sensitivity, visualization capabilities, ease of operation and fast response, which can be as one of the most hoping technologies for Trp determination and monitoring [3,13–15]. Among the numerous fluorometric sensors, the ratiometric sensor displays more excellent advantages due to its ability to effectively eliminate the influence of fluctuating excitation sources, varying probe concentrations and changes in the local environment [16,17], thereby immensely improving the accuracy, sensitivity and selectivity of determination. In general, the development of effective optical ratiometric probe with high sensitivity and selectivity is always one of the key issues that should be taken into consideration for optical ratiometric sensors. Therefore, the design of effective optical ratiometric probe with high specificity for the analyzing of Trp is of intense interest.

As a new kind of optical materials, carbon-based nanoprobes have become increasingly popular for constructing ratiometric sensors owing to their exceptional biocompatibility, controllable physicochemical and optical properties and convenient preparation [18]. Particularly, fluorine-doped carbon-based nanoprobes, as a rising star in the field of nanomaterials, lead to the exploration of a novel domain in fluorescent sensing. From the recently reported fluorine-doped carbon-based probes (Table S1), although these fluorine-doped probes possess friendly synthesis routes, and the emission properties can be tuned by changing the precursor, to our knowledge, there have been no reports of fluorine-doped carbon-based nanoprobe with dual-wavelength emission for constructing ratiometric sensors thus far. In this situation, we synthesized a novel dual-ultraviolet luminous probe based on strong electronegativity fluorine-doped carbon nanodots (FCDs) for the sensitive and specific Trp determination. The FCDs was successfully obtained by a single-step green solvothermal route using 4-fluorophenethylamine (4-FPA) as the only precursor, and do not need to add any other toxic reagents or further processing. Under 265 nm excitation, FCDs shows dual-ultraviolet-emission at 286 and 386 nm. Interestingly, the fluorescent intensities at 286 and 386 nm display a remarkable decreasing and increasing trends through the energy/electron transfer of the internal filtering effect (IFE) mechanism, respectively, following the introduction of Trp into the assay system, highlighting the potential for constructing a ratiometric sensor for Trp based on FCDs. By utilizing this IFE process, a ratiometric quantitative determination of Trp was successfully achieved, with a wide linear range from 0 to 200 μM and a detection limit of 0.31 μM. To our acquaintance, this is the first report of constructing an optical ratiometric platform for Trp detection using efficient FCDs. Scheme 1 outlines the mechanism of the developed Trp analysis concept.
Experimental details including chemicals, instruments, probe preparation, testing procedures and conditions were all described in detail in the Supplementary Materials.

Many published researches have confirmed that incorporating heteroatom into the framework of the photoluminescence (PL) carbon materials (CMs) can effectively mediate their inherently optical properties and types of surface functional groups [19,20], which can meet the performance requirements of researchers for CMs. Compared with the common doping elements such as nitrogen, sulfur and boron elements, doping CMs with the strongest electronegativity of fluorine should greatly change their inherently electronic characteristics and offer distinctive active centers, therewith producing unexpected optical properties used in the field of life analysis. Herein, fluorinated precursor, 4-FPA, was used as the F-C-N source for the preparation of PL CMs. To illustrate the effect of fluorine atom on inherent properties of CMs, comparative experiments were conducted to synthesize CMs using fluorine-free substituted ethylbenzene and phenylethylamine, and nitrogen-free substituted 4-fluoroethylbenzene as precursors under the same conditions. As shown Fig. S1, only a single emission peak of 440 nm and 340 nm was observed, respectively, using ethylbenzene (curve a) and phenylethylamine (curve b) as precursors, while two ultraviolet-emission peaks of fluorine-doped probes were observed using 4-fluoroethylbenzene (curve c) and 4-FPA (curve d) as precursors. Note that the nitrogen-doped probe synthesized with phenylethylamine has a shorter emission wavelength (λem, 340 nm) compared with the probe synthesized with ethylbenzene (λem, 440 nm), which is consistent with our previous experimental conclusion that the participation of nitrogen atom causes a blueshift in the emission wavelength of carbon-based nanoprobes, rather than redshift [21–23]. Meanwhile, compared with the fluorine-doped probe synthesized with 4-fluoroethylbenzene (λem, 286 and 400 nm), the appearance of two new wavelengths (λem, 286 and 386 nm) of the probe after co-doping with fluorine and nitrogen means that the electronic energy levels of the probe might undergo internal rearrangement due to the participation of strongest electronegativity fluorine and electron donor nitrogen atoms, the generation of novel bimodal emission nanoprobe with higher quantum efficiency (i.e., longwave emission of 386 nm originates from luminescent groups involving nitrogen, while shortwave emission of 286 nm originates from luminescent groups involving fluorine). These results further demonstrate the importance of heteroatoms properties in regulating intrinsic electronic properties of CMs.

Prior to utilizing FCDs as a novel fluorescent nanoprobe, a comprehensive investigation was conducted to assess the FCDs' appearance, elemental composition, surface chemical and optical characteristics. Firstly, the morphology of the FCDs was characterized using TEM (Fig. 1 A). The results indicate that FCDs are well-dispersed, spherical nanoparticles with average diameter ranging from 3 to 5 nm. And the HRTEM image of FCDs displays a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (inset of Fig. 1A), agreement with that of the (100) facet of graphite [24], confirming the successful synthesis of carbon nanodots. The surface functional groups of FCDs were examined using FT-IR. As depicted Fig. 1B, the surface of FCDs features two various peaks at about 1667 and 3463 cm−1 in the infrared spectrum, confirming the presence of surface carboxyl groups [25]. The vibration of the free O-H/N-H group in FCDs is observed at 3281 cm−1 [3,25]. The existence of bands at 1390 and 2932 cm−1 indicates the retention of methylene in the FCDs [26,27]. And the band at 1098 cm−1 belongs to stretching and bending vibration of C-N/C-O groups [27,28], while the absorption at 1440 and 1499 cm−1 corresponds to the aromatic ring C = C and C-H stretching vibration [26,28]. Importantly, a characteristic absorption peak of aromatic ring C-F bond is observed at 1255 cm−1 [29], and it retains the para-position with the highest polarization of F atoms towards the aromatic ring, confirmed by the peak at 661 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum.

Next, we examined XPS spectrogram for evaluating the elemental composition of the FCDs. As can be seen from Fig. 1C, the C, N, O and F peaks are observed at 285.4, 398.9, 531.6 and 684.5 eV, respectively, with relative atomic percentages of 87.57, 4.69, 7.12 and 0.62 At% (inset of Fig. 1C), respectively. There are four fitting peaks for the C1s spectrum (Fig. 1D) attributed to C-C/C = C (284.4 eV), C-N/C-O (284.7 eV), C = N/C = O (285.3 eV) and O-C = O/C-F groups (288.1 eV), respectively. In the N1s spectrum (Fig. 1E), four major peaks are observed at 399.1, 399.5, 399.8 and 400.9 eV, corresponding to pyrrole-like N, pyridine-like N, graphite-like N and amine N groups, respectively. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) also was fitted four peaks at 530.4, 531.3, 532.5 and 533.3 eV, assigning to C = O, C-OH, C-O-C and O = C-O bands, respectively. Note that the F1s spectrum (Fig. 4G) shows three fitting peaks at the binding energies of 685.8, 687.2 and 688.3 eV, which originated from the ionic C-F, semi-ionic C-F and covalent C-F bonds, respectively. The above results are completely agreement with the infrared results, which fully demonstrates that fluorine atom has been successfully introduced into FCDs frameworks. Meanwhile, thanks to the abundant hydrophilic groups such as –OH, –COOH, –NH2 and C-F groups on the surface of the FCDs, making it exhibiting excellent hydrophilicity and stability in aqueous systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the photophysical performance of the FCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we investigated the UV–vis and PL spectra. The UV–vis spectrum of FCDs (Fig. 2 A, curve a) possess three peaks centered at ∼ 233, 265 and 345 nm. The peak of 233 nm corresponds to the π-π* transition absorption of the annular conjugated system [30], while the shoulder-like peak at ∼ 265 nm can be ascribed to the n-π* transitions of C-F and C = N/C = O groups in the high-energy region, which can generate strong ultraviolet and near-ultraviolet PL emission (Fig. 2A, curve c) under 265 nm excitation (Fig. 2A, curve b). And the peak at ∼ 345 nm should be ascribed to the C = N/C = O groups n-π* transitions in the relatively low-energy region [31], which can also generate PL emission generally (Fig. S2). By changing the excitation range from 245 to 295 nm, it is clearly seen that the luminous wavelengths of the FCDs exhibited excitation-independent PL behavior. Notably, the intensities of both peaks increase with changes in excitation, but it does not change the position of the emission peak (Fig. 2B), which indicate surface state of probe determines emission performance rather than size-effect of nanoparticles. Considering the need to use this probe to build a ratio sensing platform, subsequent experiments were conducted using excitation at 265 nm. And utilizing quinine sulfate as the standard (λex = 360 nm), the relative quantum yield of the FCDs was measured and calculated to be ∼ 16.3 % (Fig. S3).

To evaluate the optical stability of FCDs under various conditions, the tendency of PL intensities (the ratio of PL386nm/PL286nm) variation at various pH values, ionic strength and storage time were conducted. As shown Figure S4A, the PL intensity of FCDs was examined in solutions with varying pH values ranging from 1.0 to 13.0, which displays that the value of PL386nm/PL286nm remains relatively stable within the pH range of 2.0–13.0. Figure S4B demonstrates that the FCDs exhibits remarkable stability even at high ionic strength, extending up to 300 mM NaCl. Moreover, Figure S4C reveals excellent long-term stability of the FCDs during extended storage periods. Note that the ratio of PL386nm/PL286nm shows a significant increase under strong acidity conditions (pH < 2), which might be due to inability of nitrogen- and oxygen-containing groups to counteract the influence of doped fluorine atom on hydrogen proton. Anyway, these findings offer compelling evidence of the exceptional stability of FCDs, positioning them as highly promising candidate for fluorescent probes in sensing applications.

To provide deeper insights into the potential applications of FCDs, particularly in Trp biosensing, the PL behavior of FCDs induced by Trp under diverse environmental pH conditions was further conducted. Fig. S5 presents alterations in the PL signals of FCDs following the introduction of 50 μM Trp at different pH conditions. The pH independence suggests that the PL behavior of the FCDs with Trp remains basically consistent across a range of pH values in the given buffer conditions. This observation is valuable in understanding the stability and reliability of the PL signals in different pH environments, indicating the potential robustness of the FCDs for applications involving pH-sensitive Trp detection. However, considering the physiological environment for detecting Trp, pH 7.4 of PBS buffer was chose as the detection environment for this system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric detection of Trp using FCDs-based sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the practicability of PL ratiometric analysis for Trp determination using the FCDs probe, we investigated the variations in PL response at various concentrations of Trp. As illustrated Fig. 3 A, the PL intensity at 386 nm of FCDs significantly increases as the Trp concentration increases, while the emission at 286 nm is decreased. This resulted in an obvious change in the PL386nm/PL286nm ratio value. As depicted Fig. 3B, a perfect linear correlation (R2 = 0.998) was obtained between the value of PL386nm/PL286nm ratio and the Trp concentration in the range of 0–200 μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) ∼0.31 μM (3σ/k), which is equivalent to or even better than before reported methods (Table S2). These results suggest that the potential use of the dual-emissive FCDs as nanoprobe for quantization of Trp in a ratiometric method.

Along with the sensitivity requirement, specificity is an important parameter for evaluating sensing performance in complex analysis system. To estimate the selectivity, the PL signal response of this sensing platform was monitored through using various potential interferences instead of Trp, such as Pro, Val, Ala, Ser, Gly, Gln, Phe, Lys, Tyr, Glu, His, Asn, Asp, Cys, Arg and Dopa. Fig. 3C presents the PL spectra of FCDs following their interaction with Trp and other interfering substances, while the Fig. 3D displays the corresponding PL intensity ratio at PL386nm/PL286nm of FCDs after their reaction with Trp and various coexisting species, respectively. Notably, a substantial increase in relative PL intensities (PL386nm/PL286nm) was observed when Trp was introduced to the FCDs. In contrast, the addition of other interfering substances to the FCDs dispersion did not result in a significant change in PL intensity. These observations support the capability of the proposed method to discriminate between Trp and the interfering agents. In addition, considering many biological specimens have autofluorescence or absorption in short wavelength emission (blue or UV emission) region, the stability of UV emitting FCDs in biological sample (Taking bovine serum samples (BSS, no Trp) as an example) was investigated. As shown in Fig. S6, the PL intensities of the probe remain basically unchanged in both emissive bands, indicating that FCDs have excellent stability in complex biological environments and are not affected by biological specimens. Then, the detection of Trp in BSS was performed to evaluate the reliability of the proposed ratiometric assay. The recovery rate is set as an evaluation criterion, which is determined by spiking various standard amounts of Trp to the serum sample. The obtained results, presented in Table 1 , displayed acceptable recoveries ranging from 96.4 % to 103.3 %, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) ranging from 2.49 % to 4.36 %. The results validate that the FCDs-based constructed ratiometric sensing strategy provides a promising alternative for the measurement of Trp in real biological samples.

Based on the presented experimental results, it has been demonstrated that the dual-emissive FCDs can effectively be employed for Trp detection using a ratiometric method. To clarify the mechanism for detecting Trp, the PL spectra were executed. As illustrated in Fig. 4 A, FCDs dispersion in the absence Trp exhibits two strong PL peaks at 286 and 386 nm (curve b) under 265 nm excitation (curve a). Meanwhile, after research, Trp itself also exhibits luminescent performance (Fig. 4A, curve d), with its excitation peak located at ∼ 286 nm (Fig. 4A, curve c). It is well-recognized that the internal filtering effect (IFE) is responsible for the excitation and/or emission photons by energy/electron absorbers within the analysis system [18,32]. Herein, the integral overlapped spectra between the PL emission at 286 nm of the FCDs and the excitation of Trp are present in this sensing platform (Fig. 4A, curves b and c), which ensures that the IFE-based energy/electron transfer occurred in a highly efficient route. That is, the emission energy/electron at 286 nm of the FCDs can be successfully converted to the excitation electron/energy of Trp when 265 nm of ultraviolet wavelength were used to excite the FCDs, leading to PL quenching at 286 nm of FCD, coupled with a prominent increase in Trp PL (Fig. 3A), which was further confirmed by an optical comparative experiment of the Trp in the absence and presence of FCDs (Fig. S7). Combined with the rapid response between the FCD and Trp (Fig. S8), these reasons fully ensure a sensitive ratiometric rapid response between FCD and Trp with the IFE-based energy/electron transfer method. Note that Trp has also two emission peaks of 346 and 386 nm under 286 nm excitation (Fig. 4A), which might be the reason for the slight blue-shift of the 386 nm emission as Trp concentration increases. To obtain further perspective into the mechanism for Trp-induced PL changes, the UV–vis measurements were carried out. As shown in Fig. S9, compared with the UV–vis spectra of pure FCDs and Trp, no new absorption peak appeared when Trp was added to the FCDs solution, illustrating non-generating of Meisenheimer-complexes between FCDs and Trp [33]. The experimental results suggest that the PL quenching and enhancing effects observed between FCDs and Trp should be attributable to the IFE-based energy/electron transfer mechanism. To further verify this mechanism, the time-resolved PL spectra of FCDs both in the nonexistence and existence of Trp were investigated. As shown Fig. 4B and Table 2 , the double-exponential decay curves of FCDs and FCDs/Trp reveal their average lifetimes of 3.40 and 2.33 ns, respectively, of which the PL lifetime of the FCDs shows significant changes with the addition of Trp. By the analysis of the PL lifetimes data revealed that the CE and kET for the FCDs in the presence of Trp (100 µM) were 31.5 % and 1.35 × 108, respectively (Table 2), further demonstrating the existence of electron transfer between FCDs and Trp. From the above results and discussion, the detection of Trp using FCDs as a ratiometric probe can be attributed to IFE-based energy/electron transfer mechanism.

In summary, a novel ratiometric optical sensor has been successfully developed for the highly sensitive and specific detection of Trp based on dual-ultraviolet-emission nanoprobe of FCDs. Notably, upon the introduction of Trp into the assay system, the PL intensity of FCDs at 286 nm decreases, while the PL intensity at 386 nm of FCDs/Trp mixed system exhibits a significant increase. The monitoring mechanism has been confirmed to be through IFE-based energy/electron transfer mechanism between the FCDs and Trp. This process can fully ensure that FCDs-based constructed sensing platform possesses the excellent selectivity and sensitivity ratiometric response in the PL ratio (PL386nm/PL286nm) for Trp and other potential interferences. The ratiometric response enabled the quantification of Trp with a low LOD of ∼ 0.31 µM. Furthermore, the FCDs was successfully utilized for the detection of Trp in real serum samples, yielding satisfactory results. Therefore, the present ratiometric optical sensor can be considered a promising detection method for monitoring the content of Trp in biological systems.

Kai-Qi Liu: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhong-Xia Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Zhen-Tao Jiang: Validation, Data curation. Feng Li: Methodology, Investigation. Heng-Ye Li: Funding acquisition, Data curation. Wei Wang: Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization. Hang Gao: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21974120, 22204075), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20191479, BK20220769), the Project of State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2309), the Jiangsu Province for the College Graduate Research and Innovation Projects (KYCX23_3470), and the Funding for School-Level Research Projects of Yancheng Institute of Technology (xjr2022012).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2024.115819.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct detection of tryptophan for rapid diagnosis of cancer cell metastasis competence by an ultra-sensitive and highly selective electrochemical biosensor
Anal. Methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.R. Majidi, P. Karami, M. Johari-Ahar, Y. Omidi, Direct detection of tryptophan for rapid diagnosis of cancer cell metastasis competence by an ultra-sensitive and highly selective electrochemical biosensor, Anal. Methods 8 (2016) 7910-7919.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Control of brain tryptophan concentration in rats on a high fat diet
Nature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.H. Hutson, P.J. Knott, G. Curzon, Control of brain tryptophan concentration in rats on a high fat diet, Nature 262 (1976) 142-143.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recyclable europium functionalized metal-organic fluorescent probe for detection of tryptophan in biological fluids and food products
Anal. Chim. Acta

Yuan, Recyclable europium functionalized metal-organic fluorescent probe for detection of tryptophan in biological fluids and food products, Anal. Chim. Acta 1180 (2021), 338897.
